questions.q: How do JWT and OAuth 2.0 relate for API security?

answer.oneLiner: OAuth 2.0 is the authorization framework that defines how a client gets an access token. JWT is a common token format used to carry that token and its claims.
answer.detail: OAuth 2.0 defines several flows for how a client application obtains a token from an authorization server. The most common flow for web apps is Authorization Code, where the user logs in at the auth server and the app receives a code to exchange for tokens. The token can be opaque (a random string the server looks up) or a JWT (self-contained with claims). JWT stands for JSON Web Token and consists of three dot-separated parts: Header, Payload, and Signature. The payload carries claims like user ID, roles, and expiry. The signature lets any resource server validate the token locally without a database lookup. OpenID Connect (OIDC) is a thin identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 that standardizes an ID token (always a JWT) to tell you who the user is. So OAuth handles the flow to get tokens, JWT is one way to format them, and OIDC adds authentication to OAuth's authorization.
keyPoints: OAuth 2.0 is the flow for obtaining tokens, not a token format
JWT is the token format: Header.Payload.Signature, signed with a key
Resource servers validate JWT signatures locally without a server roundtrip
OpenID Connect adds authentication on top of OAuth 2.0 via a standardized ID token
An opaque token requires server-side introspection; a JWT is self-validating

code.note: The framework validates the JWT signature, issuer, audience, and expiry automatically - you should never decode and trust claims without this validation.

answer.analogy: OAuth is the passport control process; JWT is the passport document itself.
redFlags: Saying OAuth and JWT are the same thing - one is a flow, one is a format
Not knowing that JWT payload is signed but not encrypted by default
Confusing OIDC with OAuth - OIDC adds authentication, OAuth is authorization only
Thinking JWT requires a server lookup to validate - it is self-contained

followUpsQA.q: Can OAuth work without JWT?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - tokens can be opaque random strings that require server-side introspection to validate, which is a round trip back to the auth server on every request.

followUpsQA.q: What does OpenID Connect add to OAuth?
followUpsQA.a: It adds an ID token (always a JWT) that tells your app who the user is, since OAuth only handles authorization, not authentication.

followUpsQA.q: Why does your API not need to call the auth server for each request when using JWT?
followUpsQA.a: The JWT is self-contained and cryptographically signed, so the resource server validates the signature locally using the public key or shared secret.

followUpsQA.q: What are the three parts of a JWT?
followUpsQA.a: Header (algorithm and type), Payload (claims like userId and roles), and Signature (HMAC or RSA hash of the first two parts).

followUpsQA.q: Is the JWT payload secret?
followUpsQA.a: No - it is Base64-encoded, not encrypted. Anyone who intercepts the token can read the claims, so never store sensitive data like passwords in the payload.

dialogue.text: Can you explain how JWT and OAuth 2.0 relate to each other for API security?

They operate at different layers. OAuth 2.0 is the authorization framework that defines the flows for how a client obtains an access token. JWT is just a token format used to carry that token and its claims. You can have OAuth without JWT, and you can use JWT outside of OAuth.
So what does the resource API actually do with the token when a request comes in?

It validates the JWT locally - checks the signature using the issuer's signing key, confirms the audience matches, and verifies the token has not expired. No round trip to the auth server is needed because the JWT is self-contained. In ASP.NET Core the AddJwtBearer middleware handles all of this automatically.
You said the payload carries claims - is that data visible to the client?

Yes, the payload is Base64-encoded, not encrypted. Anyone who intercepts the token can decode and read the claims. The signature only proves integrity, not confidentiality. So you should never store secrets or sensitive PII in the JWT payload.
Where does OpenID Connect fit into this picture?

OIDC sits on top of OAuth 2.0 and adds authentication. OAuth alone tells you what the client is allowed to do, but not who the user is. OIDC adds a standardized ID token, which is always a JWT, that carries identity claims like name and email. So OAuth handles authorization, OIDC adds the who.
If a customer's JWT is stolen, what is the blast radius and how do you contain it?

The blast radius lasts until the access token expires, which is why access tokens should be short-lived, typically 5 to 15 minutes. You pair them with a longer-lived refresh token stored in an httpOnly cookie. To contain the damage you revoke the refresh token server-side, so the attacker cannot get new access tokens after the current one expires.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the difference between Filters and Middleware? When do you use which?

answer.oneLiner: Middleware operates on the raw HTTP pipeline for every request and knows nothing about MVC. Filters run inside the MVC action pipeline and have access to the action, its arguments, and model state.
answer.detail: When a request arrives in ASP.NET Core it passes through the middleware pipeline first. Middleware sees only the raw HttpContext - it has no concept of controllers, actions, route data, or model binding. This makes it ideal for cross-cutting HTTP concerns that apply everywhere, like authentication, CORS, response compression, and logging. Filters are a feature of the MVC layer and only run when routing has matched a controller or minimal API endpoint. They execute in a defined order: Authorization filters run first and can short-circuit. Resource filters wrap model binding and are good for caching. Action filters run before and after the action method. Exception filters catch exceptions thrown by actions. Result filters run before and after the response is sent. Because filters have access to the action descriptor, bound arguments, ModelState, and the action result, they are the right tool for anything that needs MVC context, such as validating model state, auditing specific actions, or transforming action results.
keyPoints: Middleware runs for every request including static files; filters only run when MVC routing matches
Middleware sees only HttpContext; filters see the action, its arguments, ModelState, and the result
Filter execution order: Authorization, Resource, Action, Exception, Result
Use middleware for HTTP-level concerns like CORS, auth, compression, and request logging
Use filters for MVC-level concerns like model validation auditing and action-level caching

code.note: Only an action filter can access the bound action arguments - middleware runs before model binding occurs.

answer.analogy: Middleware is airport security for all passengers; filters are the checks at the boarding gate for your specific flight.
redFlags: Saying filters and middleware are interchangeable - they run at different pipeline stages
Not knowing that middleware cannot see action arguments or ModelState
Confusing the filter execution order - Authorization always runs first
Using a filter for CORS or compression - those belong in middleware

followUpsQA.q: What can a filter access that middleware cannot?
followUpsQA.a: The matched action descriptor, its bound arguments, the ModelState dictionary, and the IActionResult before it is sent to the client.

followUpsQA.q: Does middleware run for static file requests?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - middleware runs for every request. Filters only run when the MVC routing middleware has matched a controller action or endpoint.

followUpsQA.q: What is the correct filter execution order?
followUpsQA.a: Authorization, Resource, Action, Exception, Result - in that order going in, and reversed on the way out for the wrapping types.

followUpsQA.q: Why put global exception handling in middleware rather than an exception filter?
followUpsQA.a: Middleware catches exceptions from anywhere in the pipeline including other middleware, while an exception filter only catches exceptions thrown by actions and action filters.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use a Resource filter?
followUpsQA.a: For output caching at the MVC level - a resource filter wraps model binding so it can short-circuit and return a cached response before binding work is even done.

dialogue.text: What is the difference between filters and middleware in ASP.NET Core, and when do you choose one over the other?

Middleware works on the raw HTTP pipeline before MVC is involved. It sees only the HttpContext and runs for every request including static files. Filters run inside the MVC layer after routing has matched, and they have access to the action, its bound arguments, and ModelState.
Give me a concrete example where a filter is the only option.

Auditing who accessed which order. I need to read the bound orderId argument and the current user identity together. Middleware runs before model binding so the orderId is not available yet. An action filter runs after binding, so I can read ctx.ActionArguments["orderId"] and log it.
And what is a case where middleware is clearly better than a filter?

CORS, response compression, and global exception handling. These need to run for every request including static files and non-MVC endpoints. A filter would miss requests that do not hit a controller action at all.
Walk me through the filter execution order.

Authorization filters run first and can reject the request immediately. Then Resource filters wrap model binding. Then Action filters run before and after the action method. Exception filters catch any exceptions the action throws. Result filters wrap the response writing. Each wrapping type runs inbound before the inner stage and outbound after it, like middleware.
If you put global exception handling in an exception filter instead of middleware, what do you miss?

Exceptions thrown by middleware itself, or by other middleware earlier in the pipeline. The exception filter only covers exceptions from within the MVC action execution. Middleware-level exception handling wraps everything and gives full coverage.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are the different types of filters in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: There are five filter types in ASP.NET Core: Authorization, Resource, Action, Exception, and Result - each runs at a specific stage of the MVC action pipeline.
answer.detail: Filters form a mini-pipeline inside the MVC layer. Authorization filters run first, before anything else, and can reject the request with a 401 or 403 by short-circuiting. Resource filters wrap the entire remaining pipeline including model binding, making them ideal for caching because they can return a cached response before binding even starts. Action filters run immediately before and after the action method itself, so they can see and modify the bound arguments going in and the action result coming out. Exception filters catch exceptions thrown by action filters and the action method, but not exceptions from other filter types or middleware. Result filters run before and after the IActionResult is executed, so they can inspect or wrap the response. Filters can be applied globally in AddMvc options, at the controller level with an attribute, or at the action level. They are executed in the order: global, then controller, then action, with each type following the Authorization through Result sequence.
keyPoints: Authorization filters run first and can short-circuit with 401 or 403 before any other work
Resource filters wrap model binding and are ideal for response caching
Action filters see bound arguments before and the IActionResult after the action runs
Exception filters only catch exceptions from the action and action filters, not from middleware
Result filters wrap the response writing and can modify headers or the result object

code.note: A resource filter can skip model binding entirely on a cache hit, saving measurable processing work under load.

answer.analogy: Filters are checkpoints at each floor of a building - you must pass each one in order before reaching the executive suite.
redFlags: Saying exception filters catch all exceptions - they miss middleware exceptions and resource filter exceptions
Not knowing that Resource filters run around model binding, not just around the action
Confusing Authorization filters with the Authorize attribute - the attribute triggers Authorization filters
Not knowing the correct execution order: Authorization, Resource, Action, Exception, Result

followUpsQA.q: Which filter type runs first in the pipeline?
followUpsQA.a: Authorization filters run first and can reject the request immediately without running model binding or the action.

followUpsQA.q: Why is a Resource filter better than an Action filter for caching?
followUpsQA.a: A Resource filter wraps model binding so it can short-circuit and return a cached result before binding happens, saving that processing cost entirely.

followUpsQA.q: What exceptions does an Exception filter NOT catch?
followUpsQA.a: Exceptions thrown by middleware, by Authorization filters, or by Resource filters - the Exception filter scope is limited to the action method and Action filters.

followUpsQA.q: How do you apply a filter globally?
followUpsQA.a: In the AddControllers or AddMvc options call: builder.Services.AddControllers(o => o.Filters.Add<MyFilter>()).

followUpsQA.q: Can a filter run asynchronously?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - implement IAsyncActionFilter or IAsyncResultFilter instead of the synchronous interface to use async and await inside the filter.

dialogue.text: Can you walk me through the different types of filters in ASP.NET Core and what each one is for?

There are five types. Authorization filters run first and short-circuit unauthorized requests before any other work. Resource filters wrap model binding and are ideal for caching. Action filters run before and after the action method. Exception filters catch action-level exceptions. Result filters wrap the response writing.
You mentioned Resource filters are ideal for caching - why not use an Action filter for that?

Because an Action filter runs after model binding has already happened. If I have a cache hit, I have already done the binding work for nothing. A Resource filter wraps model binding so I can return the cached result before binding starts, which is a real saving under load on a high-traffic product catalog endpoint.
What does an Exception filter not catch that surprises developers?

Exceptions from middleware and from Authorization or Resource filters. The Exception filter scope is only the action method and Action filters. If an exception is thrown by middleware earlier in the pipeline, the Exception filter never sees it. That is why you still need a middleware-level exception handler for full coverage.
How would you apply a filter to every controller without decorating each one?

Register it globally: builder.Services.AddControllers(o => o.Filters.Add<AuditFilter>()). It then runs for every matched action in the app. You can still override or disable it on individual controllers or actions with specific attributes if needed.
If a Result filter throws an exception, what handles it?

An Exception filter would not catch it because Result filters run after Action filters and Exception filters in the pipeline sequence. The exception propagates out of the MVC layer and is caught by the global middleware exception handler, which is one more reason to always have middleware-level exception handling in place.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are Action Results in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: An IActionResult represents the HTTP response an action method returns. Helper methods like Ok(), CreatedAtAction(), BadRequest(), NotFound(), and NoContent() pair the correct status code with the response payload.
answer.detail: Every controller action in ASP.NET Core returns some representation of an HTTP response. The IActionResult interface abstracts that response, allowing the framework to execute it - setting status codes, writing headers, and serializing the body. The ControllerBase class provides helper methods that create the right IActionResult for common situations. Ok(value) produces a 200 with the serialized value as JSON. CreatedAtAction(action, routeValues, value) produces a 201 with a Location header pointing at the newly created resource, which is the correct REST response for a POST that creates something. BadRequest(modelState) produces a 400 and can serialize validation errors. NotFound() produces a 404. NoContent() produces a 204 with no body, which is the correct response for a successful DELETE. Using ActionResult<T> combines the typed return value with the ability to return different IActionResults from the same action, and it improves Swagger documentation because the framework knows the success type.
keyPoints: Ok(value) returns 200 with serialized body; CreatedAtAction returns 201 with a Location header
NoContent() is the correct 204 response for successful DELETE operations
ActionResult<T> lets you return a typed value or an IActionResult from the same action
BadRequest() with ModelState serializes validation errors automatically with ApiController
Never return 200 with an error body - use the appropriate 4xx or 5xx status code

code.note: CreatedAtAction returns 201 with a Location header pointing to the new resource - never use Ok() for a successful create.

answer.analogy: Action results are official reply envelopes - pick the right one so the client knows exactly what happened.
redFlags: Returning 200 for everything including errors - this breaks clients that rely on status codes for retry and error handling
Using Ok() for a successful POST create instead of CreatedAtAction with 201
Not knowing that NoContent() is the correct response for DELETE, not Ok() with null
Confusing Unauthorized() (401) and Forbid() (403) - use them for the correct scenarios

followUpsQA.q: What does CreatedAtAction give you that Ok does not?
followUpsQA.a: A 201 status code plus a Location header pointing to the URL of the newly created resource, which is the correct REST semantics for a successful POST.

followUpsQA.q: What is the correct response for a successful DELETE?
followUpsQA.a: NoContent() - a 204 status with no response body, indicating the operation succeeded and there is nothing to return.

followUpsQA.q: Why use ActionResult<T> instead of IActionResult?
followUpsQA.a: It communicates the success type to Swagger so documentation is generated correctly, and it allows returning either the typed value or an error IActionResult from the same action signature.

followUpsQA.q: What status code should you return when a resource is not found?
followUpsQA.a: 404 via NotFound() - never 200 with an empty body or null, and never 500 for a missing record.

followUpsQA.q: How does BadRequest work with ApiController?
followUpsQA.a: With the ApiController attribute, invalid ModelState automatically returns a 400 with a ProblemDetails body containing the validation errors before your action even runs.

dialogue.text: Can you explain what action results are in ASP.NET Core and how they work?

IActionResult is an abstraction for the HTTP response an action method produces. The framework executes it to set the status code, write headers, and serialize the body. ControllerBase provides helper methods like Ok(), CreatedAtAction(), BadRequest(), and NotFound() that create the right response for common situations.
You are building an e-commerce API. A customer places an order successfully. What do you return and why?

CreatedAtAction pointing to the GetOrder endpoint with the new order ID, returning 201 and a Location header. Not Ok() with 200, because 201 is the correct REST semantic for a successful resource creation and the Location header tells the client where to retrieve the new order.
What is the difference between returning Unauthorized() and Forbid()?

Unauthorized() returns 401, meaning the user is not authenticated - no valid token. Forbid() returns 403, meaning the user is authenticated but does not have permission. Returning 401 when you mean 403 confuses clients because they will retry authentication even though their credentials are fine.
Why would you use ActionResult<T> instead of plain IActionResult?

ActionResult<T> lets me return either the typed value directly or an IActionResult for error cases from the same action. It also improves Swagger output because the framework knows the success response type and generates accurate schema documentation without extra annotations.
In a legacy codebase every endpoint returns Ok() even for errors, with different message strings in the body. What is the practical problem?

API clients and load balancers rely on HTTP status codes to understand what happened. Returning 200 for errors means monitoring alerts never fire, client retry logic never triggers, and error handling requires parsing the body for every response. It breaks the HTTP contract that REST APIs are built on.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is CORS and how do you enable it?

answer.oneLiner: CORS is a browser security mechanism that blocks JavaScript from reading responses from a different origin unless the server explicitly opts in via response headers. In ASP.NET Core you define a named policy and add UseCors to the middleware pipeline.
answer.detail: The Same-Origin Policy is a browser rule that prevents JavaScript on one origin (scheme plus host plus port) from reading responses from a different origin. This protects users from malicious sites reading their bank data. CORS is the standard mechanism that lets a server relax this restriction by including specific response headers. For simple requests like GET, the browser adds an Origin header and checks if the response includes Access-Control-Allow-Origin with a matching value. For non-simple requests such as POST with a JSON body or custom headers, the browser first sends a preflight OPTIONS request. The server must respond to the preflight with the allowed origins, methods, and headers, and only then does the browser send the real request. Critically, CORS is enforced by the browser, not the server. A server cannot block a non-browser HTTP client like Postman just with CORS headers. In ASP.NET Core you call builder.Services.AddCors and define a named policy specifying allowed origins, headers, and methods. Then you call app.UseCors with the policy name, positioning it after UseRouting and before UseAuthorization so preflight requests are handled correctly.
keyPoints: CORS is enforced by the browser only - Postman and server-to-server calls ignore it completely
Preflight OPTIONS request is sent by the browser before non-simple cross-origin requests
UseCors must go after UseRouting and before UseAuthorization in the pipeline
Never combine AllowAnyOrigin with AllowCredentials - the browser blocks this combination
CORS headers tell the browser whether to allow JavaScript to read the response, not whether to block the request

code.note: AllowAnyOrigin with AllowCredentials is invalid and blocked by browsers - always specify exact origins when using credentials.

answer.analogy: CORS is the club bouncer who only lets in guests from the approved list - but only browsers obey the list.
redFlags: Saying the server blocks non-browser clients with CORS - CORS is browser-only enforcement
Not knowing that AllowAnyOrigin and AllowCredentials cannot be combined
Placing UseCors before UseRouting - endpoint-specific CORS policies will not work correctly
Thinking CORS is a server-side security measure that protects the API from unauthorized access

followUpsQA.q: Who enforces CORS - the browser or the server?
followUpsQA.a: The browser. The server just adds response headers saying what is allowed. A non-browser client like Postman or a server-to-server HTTP call completely ignores CORS headers.

followUpsQA.q: What is a preflight request?
followUpsQA.a: An automatic OPTIONS request the browser sends before a non-simple cross-origin request to check if the server allows it. If the server does not respond correctly, the browser blocks the real request.

followUpsQA.q: Why can you not combine AllowAnyOrigin with AllowCredentials?
followUpsQA.a: Browsers block this combination as a security measure. When credentials are involved, you must specify exact origins so users are not tricked into sending their cookies to any site.

followUpsQA.q: Where does UseCors belong in the middleware pipeline?
followUpsQA.a: After UseRouting and before UseAuthorization, so the framework knows which endpoint is matched and can apply the correct endpoint-specific CORS policy.

followUpsQA.q: Can CORS protect a server-side API from unauthorized JavaScript access?
followUpsQA.a: No - CORS only controls whether the browser lets JavaScript read the response. The API still receives the request and must apply its own authentication and authorization to protect data.

dialogue.text: Can you explain what CORS is and how you enable it in ASP.NET Core?

CORS stands for Cross-Origin Resource Sharing. It is a browser security mechanism that blocks JavaScript from reading responses from a different origin unless the server opts in by returning specific response headers. In ASP.NET Core you define a named policy in AddCors and call UseCors in the middleware pipeline.
You said the browser blocks the response - does the request still reach the server?

For simple requests yes, the browser sends the request and the server processes it fully. The browser then checks the CORS headers on the response and blocks the JavaScript from reading it if the origin is not allowed. For non-simple requests the browser sends a preflight OPTIONS request first and only proceeds if the server responds correctly.
A developer suggests using AllowAnyOrigin to fix a CORS issue in production. What is your response?

I would push back. AllowAnyOrigin means any website can make JavaScript calls to our API and read the responses, which defeats the protection the Same-Origin Policy provides. For production I would use WithOrigins and specify the exact domains of our frontend applications, such as our customer portal and admin dashboard.
What happens if you put UseCors before UseRouting in the pipeline?

The CORS middleware runs before routing has matched an endpoint, so it cannot determine which endpoint-specific CORS policy to apply. Preflight responses may be missing or incorrect, causing browsers to block legitimate cross-origin requests even when the API is supposed to allow them.
If an API endpoint has no CORS headers and someone calls it from Postman, what happens?

Postman calls it successfully and gets the full response. CORS is enforced by browsers, not servers. A non-browser client ignores CORS headers completely. This is an important distinction - CORS does not protect the API from unauthorized access; authentication and authorization do that.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the difference between role-based and policy-based authorization?

answer.oneLiner: Role-based authorization checks if the user has a specific role claim. Policy-based authorization evaluates flexible requirements built from any claims or custom logic, and it scales better as access rules become more complex.
answer.detail: Role-based authorization is a simple form of claim checking where you declare [Authorize(Roles="Admin")] and the framework checks whether the user's ClaimsPrincipal contains a role claim with that value. It is easy to set up but becomes fragile when rules get compound. A requirement like 'user is a sales manager AND in the EMEA region AND the account belongs to their team' cannot be expressed cleanly with roles alone. You end up creating many granular role strings and the codebase becomes hard to audit. Policy-based authorization solves this by letting you define named policies in AddAuthorization. A policy is built from one or more IAuthorizationRequirement objects. For each requirement you write an AuthorizationHandler that inspects the ClaimsPrincipal and optionally the resource being accessed, then calls context.Succeed() or context.Fail(). This gives you full flexibility, testability, and a central place to read all access rules. The endpoint uses [Authorize(Policy="CanEditOrder")] and the framework evaluates all handlers for that policy's requirements.
keyPoints: Role-based uses [Authorize(Roles="Admin")] and checks a single role claim value
Policy-based uses [Authorize(Policy="PolicyName")] and evaluates IAuthorizationRequirement handlers
Policies can combine multiple claims, resource ownership checks, and custom async logic
Custom policies require an IAuthorizationRequirement and a matching AuthorizationHandler
Policy-based is testable and auditable - all access rules are in one registered location

code.note: A policy centralizes complex access rules in one place and makes them independently testable, versus scattering role strings across controllers.

answer.analogy: Role-based is a guest list by job title; policy-based is a custom rule checking title, region, and ownership together.
redFlags: Saying role-based and policy-based are interchangeable - roles are simple, policies are composable
Not knowing that custom policies require IAuthorizationRequirement and AuthorizationHandler
Creating dozens of granular roles to handle compound access rules instead of using policies
Confusing ClaimsPrincipal roles with database roles - the role value in the JWT claim must match exactly

followUpsQA.q: What are the components of a custom authorization policy?
followUpsQA.a: An IAuthorizationRequirement that carries the rule parameters, and an AuthorizationHandler that evaluates the ClaimsPrincipal against the requirement and calls context.Succeed() or context.Fail().

followUpsQA.q: When should you still use role-based authorization?
followUpsQA.a: When the access rule is a single simple check like 'user must be Admin' with no compound logic. It is the right tool when roles map cleanly to the business permission model.

followUpsQA.q: Can a policy check the actual resource being accessed, not just the user's claims?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - resource-based authorization passes the resource object to the AuthorizationHandler so you can check ownership, like whether the logged-in user owns the order they are trying to edit.

followUpsQA.q: How do you register a custom authorization handler?
followUpsQA.a: Register it with builder.Services.AddSingleton<IAuthorizationHandler, RegionMatchHandler>() and the framework calls it when evaluating any policy containing the matching requirement.

followUpsQA.q: What does [Authorize] with no arguments do?
followUpsQA.a: It requires the user to be authenticated using any configured scheme, but applies no role or policy check - it only ensures User.Identity.IsAuthenticated is true.

dialogue.text: Can you explain the difference between role-based and policy-based authorization and when you would use each?

Role-based is a simple check: [Authorize(Roles="Admin")] verifies that the user's token contains an Admin role claim. Policy-based defines named policies backed by requirement handlers that can evaluate any combination of claims, resource ownership, or custom async logic.
When does role-based authorization start to break down in a real system?

When access rules become compound. For example, a sales manager in the EMEA region can only edit orders assigned to their team. You cannot express that cleanly with roles without creating strings like SalesManagerEMEA, which causes role explosion and makes the system hard to audit and maintain.
Walk me through how you would implement a custom policy for that scenario.

I would define a RegionalManagerRequirement implementing IAuthorizationRequirement. Then write a RegionalManagerHandler extending AuthorizationHandler<RegionalManagerRequirement, SalesOrder> that checks the user's region claim matches the order's region and the department claim is Sales. Register the handler as a singleton and define the policy in AddAuthorization.
How do you test a custom authorization handler in isolation?

I create an AuthorizationHandlerContext with a mock ClaimsPrincipal and the resource object, then call HandleAsync on the handler directly in a unit test. I assert that context.HasSucceeded is true for permitted cases and false for denied ones. No HTTP request needed - the handler is a plain testable class.
What is the difference between using [Authorize(Policy="X")] on the action versus calling IAuthorizationService.AuthorizeAsync inside the action body?

The attribute runs as an Authorization filter before the action executes and returns 401 or 403 automatically. IAuthorizationService.AuthorizeAsync is called manually inside the action when you need to check authorization against a specific resource you have just loaded from the database, like verifying the current user owns the order before allowing the edit.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: Why use refresh tokens with JWTs?

answer.oneLiner: Access tokens are kept short-lived to limit damage if stolen. A longer-lived refresh token lets the client silently obtain a new access token without forcing the user to log in again.
answer.detail: A JWT access token is self-contained and stateless, which means the server cannot revoke it once issued. If an access token is stolen, the attacker can use it until it expires. The solution is to keep access tokens very short-lived, typically 5 to 15 minutes, so the window of opportunity is small. But if the user had to log in again every 10 minutes that would be a terrible experience. The refresh token solves this. It is a long-lived, opaque credential - typically a random string - stored server-side in a database. When the access token expires, the client sends the refresh token to a dedicated token endpoint, the server validates it against the database, and issues a new access token. Because the refresh token is stored server-side it can be revoked instantly, for example when the user logs out or when a compromise is detected. Rotation is the practice of issuing a new refresh token on each use and invalidating the old one immediately. This means that if a refresh token is stolen, it can only be used once before the legitimate client's next use invalidates it, and the server can detect the reuse and revoke the entire session. The refresh token should always be stored in an httpOnly Secure cookie so JavaScript cannot read it.
keyPoints: Access tokens should be short-lived (5-15 minutes) to limit the blast radius if stolen
Refresh tokens are stored server-side and are revocable, unlike self-contained JWTs
Refresh token rotation issues a new refresh token on each use and treats reuse as a compromise signal
Refresh tokens must be stored in httpOnly Secure cookies to prevent JavaScript access
On logout, revoke the refresh token server-side so no new access tokens can be minted

code.note: The access token lives in client memory for 10 minutes. The refresh token sits in an httpOnly cookie that JavaScript cannot read.

answer.analogy: The access token is your daily badge; the refresh token is the HR system that prints you a new badge each morning.
redFlags: Storing the refresh token in localStorage - JavaScript can read it and XSS attacks can steal it
Not rotating refresh tokens - a stolen refresh token works forever without rotation
Making access tokens long-lived to avoid implementing refresh tokens - this defeats the purpose
Not revoking refresh tokens on logout - the session stays alive on the server indefinitely

followUpsQA.q: Why cannot you revoke a JWT access token like you can a session cookie?
followUpsQA.a: A JWT is self-contained and stateless. The server validates it without a database lookup, so there is no central record to mark as revoked. You would have to maintain a denylist, which reintroduces server state.

followUpsQA.q: What is refresh token rotation and why does it matter?
followUpsQA.a: On each use a new refresh token is issued and the old one is immediately invalidated. If a stolen token is used by an attacker, the legitimate client's next refresh will fail, alerting the server to a compromise and allowing it to revoke the entire session.

followUpsQA.q: Where should the refresh token be stored on the browser client?
followUpsQA.a: In an httpOnly Secure cookie with SameSite=Strict. This prevents JavaScript from reading it, protecting against XSS attacks. The access token stays in JavaScript memory, not localStorage.

followUpsQA.q: How do you handle logout with refresh tokens?
followUpsQA.a: Delete the refresh token from the server-side database and clear the httpOnly cookie. Without the refresh token the client cannot mint new access tokens, so the session ends when the current access token expires.

followUpsQA.q: What is the recommended access token lifetime?
followUpsQA.a: 5 to 15 minutes. Short enough to limit the damage if stolen, but long enough to avoid constant refresh calls during normal user activity.

dialogue.text: Why do we use refresh tokens alongside JWTs, and how does the whole flow work?

JWTs are stateless so you cannot revoke them once issued. To limit the damage if a token is stolen you keep access tokens very short-lived, maybe 10 minutes. A refresh token lets the client silently get a new access token when the current one expires, without asking the user to log in again.
How does having a refresh token give you revocation if the access token itself still cannot be revoked?

The access token still expires on its own schedule - you cannot cancel it mid-life. But by revoking the refresh token server-side you prevent any new access tokens being issued after the current one expires. So effective revocation takes at most one access token lifetime, typically 10 minutes.
What is refresh token rotation and how does it detect a stolen token?

On each successful refresh you issue a new refresh token and immediately invalidate the old one. If an attacker has stolen the token and uses it, the legitimate client's next refresh fails because the token was already consumed. The server sees the reuse of an invalidated token and can revoke the entire user session as a security response.
Where would you store the refresh token in a browser-based application and why?

An httpOnly Secure cookie with SameSite=Strict. The httpOnly flag means JavaScript cannot access it at all, so an XSS attack cannot steal it via document.cookie. The Secure flag ensures it only travels over HTTPS. The short-lived access token stays in JavaScript memory, never in localStorage.
A user clicks logout but reports they are still logged in 20 minutes later from another device that had the refresh token. What went wrong?

The logout endpoint only cleared the cookie on the current device but did not revoke the refresh token record in the server database. Any device that still holds a copy of the refresh token can continue minting new access tokens. Proper logout must delete the refresh token server-side so no device can use it, then clear all client-side tokens.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
